revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''節折'''（よおり）は、[[宮中祭祀]]のひとつ。毎年[[6月]]と[[12月]]の[[晦日]]に行われる。'''御贖の儀'''（みあがの…」
revision.text:
'''節折'''（よおり）は、[[宮中祭祀]]のひとつ。毎年[[6月]]と[[12月]]の[[晦日]]に行われる。'''御贖の儀'''（みあがのぎ）ともいう。
6月と12月の晦日には宮中にて[[大祓]]が行われるが、この日の夜に[[天皇]]、[[皇后]]、[[皇太子]]のために斎行される[[祓い]]の儀式である<ref>[[ブリタニカ国際大百科事典]]</ref>。
[[神祇官]]がすすめる[[竹|小竹]]9本を使い、身長および両肩から足まで、胸から左右の手先まで、左右の腰から足まで、左右の膝から足までのそれぞれの長さを測り、その箇所を折ることから「節折」の名がある。こののち神祇官の宮主が祓いをした<ref>ブリタニカ国際大百科事典</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}

{{皇室祭祀}} '''節折'''（よおり）は、[[宮中祭祀]]のひとつ。毎年[[6月]]と[[12月]]の[[晦日]]に行われる。'''御贖の儀'''（みあがのぎ）ともいう。
6月と12月の晦日には宮中にて[[大祓]]が行われるが、この日の夜に[[天皇]]、[[皇后]]、[[皇太子]]のために斎行される[[祓い]]の儀式である<ref>[[ブリタニカ国際大百科事典]]</ref>。
[[神祇官]]がすすめる[[竹|小竹]]9本を使い、身長および両肩から足まで、胸から左右の手先まで、左右の腰から足まで、左右の膝から足までのそれぞれの長さを測り、その箇所を折ることから「節折」の名がある。こののち神祇官の宮主が祓いをした<ref>ブリタニカ国際大百科事典</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *[[皇室の儀式]] *[[宮中祭祀]] *[[大祓]] *[[神祇官]] *[[祓い]]

{{皇室祭祀}} '''節折'''（よおり）は、[[宮中祭祀]]のひとつ。毎年[[6月]]と[[12月]]の[[晦日]]に行われる。'''御贖の儀'''（みあがのぎ）ともいう。
6月と12月の晦日には宮中にて[[大祓]]が行われるが、この日の夜に[[天皇]]、[[皇后]]、[[皇太子]]のために斎行される[[祓い]]の儀式である<ref>[[ブリタニカ国際大百科事典]]</ref>。
[[神祇官]]がすすめる[[竹|小竹]]9本を使い、身長および両肩から足まで、胸から左右の手先まで、左右の腰から足まで、左右の膝から足までのそれぞれの長さを測り、その箇所を折ることから「節折」の名がある。こののち神祇官の宮主が祓いをした<ref>ブリタニカ国際大百科事典</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[皇室の儀式]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[大祓]] * [[神祇官]] * [[祓い]]
{{デフォルトソート:よおり}} [[Category:皇室の儀式]] [[Category:6月]] [[Category:12月]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[ja:節折]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

{{ill|Yo-ori|lt=Yo-ori|fr|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|zh|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} (節折) is a cleansing ceremony that is an essential part of the intricate ritual schedule observed in the Japanese Imperial Court. This biannual ritual plays a role, in purifying the Emperor emphasizing the enduring importance of purification in both Shinto beliefs and imperial customs. Originating in 1871 the Yo-ori ceremonies are held twice a year on June 30th and December 31st at the Takenoma Hall located within the Imperial Palace.<ref name="Hardacre-2016">{{Cite book|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2016-11-11|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|edition=Illustrated|location=New York (N.Y.)|language=English}}</ref>{{rp|362-368}}

== Overview ==
The ceremony involves the Emperor, a Chamberlain and a Naishōten highlighting its significance and the exclusive participants involved. The Yo-ori rituals are one aspect of ceremonies that encompass agricultural traditions, ancestral rites, astrological observations and marking important dates on the calendar. The focus on purification seen in both the Ōharae and Yo-ori rituals underscores an aspect of Shinto practice; maintaining purity through cleansing.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Context and History ==
The introduction of Yo-ori rites took place during a period of changes in imperial rituals following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868. There was an effort to restructure and systematize the rituals linked to the Imperial Court during this time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

The Council of Divinities was downgraded to a position in 1871 shifting the focus, towards ritual practices within the imperial household.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Purification became more important to prepare for the ritual of {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae-shiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} became more prominent in the Meiji restoration.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362}}

During this period Emperor Jinmus significance in rhetoric influenced the development and observance of rituals like Yo-ori. The reevaluation and elevation of Jinmus role not legitimized the institution but also promoted national unity and identity.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Rituals such as Yo-ori, Ōharae, Shihōhai and Niinamesai showcase the deep rooted traditions and enduring importance of Shinto practices in the Japanese Imperial Court context. These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

[[ja:節折]][[fr:Yoori]]

These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[ja:節折]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

{{ill|Yo-ori|lt=Yo-ori|fr|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|zh|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} (節折) is a cleansing ceremony that is an essential part of the intricate ritual schedule observed in the Japanese Imperial Court. This biannual ritual plays a role, in purifying the Emperor emphasizing the enduring importance of purification in both Shinto beliefs and imperial customs. Originating in 1871 the Yo-ori ceremonies are held twice a year on June 30th and December 31st at the Takenoma Hall located within the Imperial Palace.<ref name="Hardacre-2016">{{Cite book|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2016-11-11|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|edition=Illustrated|location=New York (N.Y.)|language=English}}</ref>{{rp|362-368}}

== Overview ==
The ceremony involves the Emperor, a Chamberlain and a Naishōten highlighting its significance and the exclusive participants involved. The Yo-ori rituals are one aspect of ceremonies that encompass agricultural traditions, ancestral rites, astrological observations and marking important dates on the calendar. The focus on purification seen in both the Ōharae and Yo-ori rituals underscores an aspect of Shinto practice; maintaining purity through cleansing.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Context and History ==
The introduction of Yo-ori rites took place during a period of changes in imperial rituals following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868. There was an effort to restructure and systematize the rituals linked to the Imperial Court during this time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

The Council of Divinities was downgraded to a position in 1871 shifting the focus, towards ritual practices within the imperial household.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Purification became more important to prepare for the ritual of {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae-shiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} became more prominent in the Meiji restoration.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362}}

During this period Emperor Jinmus significance in rhetoric influenced the development and observance of rituals like Yo-ori. The reevaluation and elevation of Jinmus role not legitimized the institution but also promoted national unity and identity.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Rituals such as Yo-ori, Ōharae, Shihōhai and Niinamesai showcase the deep rooted traditions and enduring importance of Shinto practices in the Japanese Imperial Court context. These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

[[ja:節折]]
{{wikidata link|Q109362957}}

[[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[ja:節折]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

{{ill|Yo-ori|lt=Yo-ori|fr|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|zh|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} (節折) is a cleansing ceremony that is an essential part of the intricate ritual schedule observed in the Japanese Imperial Court. This biannual ritual plays a role, in purifying the Emperor emphasizing the enduring importance of purification in both Shinto beliefs and imperial customs. Originating in 1871 the Yo-ori ceremonies are held twice a year on June 30th and December 31st at the Takenoma Hall located within the Imperial Palace.<ref name="Hardacre-2016">{{Cite book|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2016-11-11|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|edition=Illustrated|location=New York (N.Y.)|language=English}}</ref>{{rp|362-368}}

== Overview ==
The ceremony involves the Emperor, a Chamberlain and a Naishōten highlighting its significance and the exclusive participants involved. The Yo-ori rituals are one aspect of ceremonies that encompass agricultural traditions, ancestral rites, astrological observations and marking important dates on the calendar. The focus on purification seen in both the Ōharae and Yo-ori rituals underscores an aspect of Shinto practice; maintaining purity through cleansing.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Context and History ==
The introduction of Yo-ori rites took place during a period of changes in imperial rituals following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868. There was an effort to restructure and systematize the rituals linked to the Imperial Court during this time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

The Council of Divinities was downgraded to a position in 1871 shifting the focus, towards ritual practices within the imperial household.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Purification became more important to prepare for the ritual of {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae-shiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} became more prominent in the Meiji restoration.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362}}

During this period Emperor Jinmus significance in rhetoric influenced the development and observance of rituals like Yo-ori. The reevaluation and elevation of Jinmus role not legitimized the institution but also promoted national unity and identity.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Rituals such as Yo-ori, Ōharae, Shihōhai and Niinamesai showcase the deep rooted traditions and enduring importance of Shinto practices in the Japanese Imperial Court context. These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

[[ja:節折]][[fr:Yoori]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[ja:節折]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

{{ill|Yo-ori|lt=Yo-ori|fr|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|zh|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} (節折) is a cleansing ceremony that is an essential part of the intricate ritual schedule observed in the Japanese Imperial Court. This biannual ritual plays a role, in purifying the Emperor emphasizing the enduring importance of purification in both Shinto beliefs and imperial customs. Originating in 1871 the Yo-ori ceremonies are held twice a year on June 30th and December 31st at the Takenoma Hall located within the Imperial Palace.<ref name="Hardacre-2016">{{Cite book|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2016-11-11|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|edition=Illustrated|location=New York (N.Y.)|language=English}}</ref>{{rp|362-368}}

== Overview ==
The ceremony involves the Emperor, a Chamberlain and a Naishōten highlighting its significance and the exclusive participants involved. The Yo-ori rituals are one aspect of ceremonies that encompass agricultural traditions, ancestral rites, astrological observations and marking important dates on the calendar. The focus on purification seen in both the Ōharae and Yo-ori rituals underscores an aspect of Shinto practice; maintaining purity through cleansing.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Context and History ==
The introduction of Yo-ori rites took place during a period of changes in imperial rituals following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868. There was an effort to restructure and systematize the rituals linked to the Imperial Court during this time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

The Council of Divinities was downgraded to a position in 1871 shifting the focus, towards ritual practices within the imperial household.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Purification became more important to prepare for the ritual of {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae-shiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} became more prominent in the Meiji restoration.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362}}

During this period Emperor Jinmus significance in rhetoric influenced the development and observance of rituals like Yo-ori. The reevaluation and elevation of Jinmus role not legitimized the institution but also promoted national unity and identity.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Rituals such as Yo-ori, Ōharae, Shihōhai and Niinamesai showcase the deep rooted traditions and enduring importance of Shinto practices in the Japanese Imperial Court context. These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

[[ja:節折]][[fr:Yoori]]

People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[ja:節折]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

{{ill|Yo-ori|lt=Yo-ori|fr|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|zh|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} (節折) is a cleansing ceremony that is an essential part of the intricate ritual schedule observed in the Japanese Imperial Court. This biannual ritual plays a role, in purifying the Emperor emphasizing the enduring importance of purification in both Shinto beliefs and imperial customs. Originating in 1871 the Yo-ori ceremonies are held twice a year on June 30th and December 31st at the Takenoma Hall located within the Imperial Palace.<ref name="Hardacre-2016">{{Cite book|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2016-11-11|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|edition=Illustrated|location=New York (N.Y.)|language=English}}</ref>{{rp|362-368}}

== Overview ==
The ceremony involves the Emperor, a Chamberlain and a Naishōten highlighting its significance and the exclusive participants involved. The Yo-ori rituals are one aspect of ceremonies that encompass agricultural traditions, ancestral rites, astrological observations and marking important dates on the calendar. The focus on purification seen in both the Ōharae and Yo-ori rituals underscores an aspect of Shinto practice; maintaining purity through cleansing.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Context and History ==
The introduction of Yo-ori rites took place during a period of changes in imperial rituals following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868. There was an effort to restructure and systematize the rituals linked to the Imperial Court during this time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

The Council of Divinities was downgraded to a position in 1871 shifting the focus, towards ritual practices within the imperial household.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Purification became more important to prepare for the ritual of {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae-shiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} became more prominent in the Meiji restoration.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362}}

During this period Emperor Jinmus significance in rhetoric influenced the development and observance of rituals like Yo-ori. The reevaluation and elevation of Jinmus role not legitimized the institution but also promoted national unity and identity.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Rituals such as Yo-ori, Ōharae, Shihōhai and Niinamesai showcase the deep rooted traditions and enduring importance of Shinto practices in the Japanese Imperial Court context. These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

[[ja:節折]][[fr:Yoori]]

{{notelist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Yoori]][[ja:節折]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[ja:節折]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

{{ill|Yo-ori|lt=Yo-ori|fr|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|zh|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} (節折) is a cleansing ceremony that is an essential part of the intricate ritual schedule observed in the Japanese Imperial Court. This biannual ritual plays a role, in purifying the Emperor emphasizing the enduring importance of purification in both Shinto beliefs and imperial customs. Originating in 1871 the Yo-ori ceremonies are held twice a year on June 30th and December 31st at the Takenoma Hall located within the Imperial Palace.<ref name="Hardacre-2016">{{Cite book|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2016-11-11|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|edition=Illustrated|location=New York (N.Y.)|language=English}}</ref>{{rp|362-368}}

== Overview ==
The ceremony involves the Emperor, a Chamberlain and a Naishōten highlighting its significance and the exclusive participants involved. The Yo-ori rituals are one aspect of ceremonies that encompass agricultural traditions, ancestral rites, astrological observations and marking important dates on the calendar. The focus on purification seen in both the Ōharae and Yo-ori rituals underscores an aspect of Shinto practice; maintaining purity through cleansing.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Context and History ==
The introduction of Yo-ori rites took place during a period of changes in imperial rituals following the Meiji Restoration, in 1868. There was an effort to restructure and systematize the rituals linked to the Imperial Court during this time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

The Council of Divinities was downgraded to a position in 1871 shifting the focus, towards ritual practices within the imperial household.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Purification became more important to prepare for the ritual of {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōharae-shiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Yo-ori|ja|節折|1=Yo-ori|2=ja|3=節折|4=fr|5=Yo-ori|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|13=User:Immanuelle/Yo-ori|qq=}} became more prominent in the Meiji restoration.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362}}

During this period Emperor Jinmus significance in rhetoric influenced the development and observance of rituals like Yo-ori. The reevaluation and elevation of Jinmus role not legitimized the institution but also promoted national unity and identity.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Rituals such as Yo-ori, Ōharae, Shihōhai and Niinamesai showcase the deep rooted traditions and enduring importance of Shinto practices in the Japanese Imperial Court context. These ceremonies serve purposes; they uphold the Emperors stature preserve spiritual purity within the imperial family and nation and demonstrate cultural and religious traditions continuity over time.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

== Minazuki-barae ==
People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>

[[ja:節折]][[fr:Yoori]]

==References==
{{reflist}}

{{notelist}}

}}

Through participation, in Yo-ori and similar ceremonies the Emperor and other royals engage in acts of reverence and purification believed to safeguard the nations well being.In Japans tradition the ceremonial calendar demonstrates the connection, among ritual practices, governance structures and spiritual beliefs through its elaborate timetable and hierarchical ceremonies.<ref name="Hardacre-2016" />{{rp|362-368}}

People often mix up the Ōharae in the sixth month with Minazuki-barae (水無月祓), another purification rite that takes place at that time. However, each had its own purpose and importance in court rituals.<ref name="Namiki">{{Cite web|last=Namiki|first=Kazuko|title=Ōharae|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9119|access-date=9 November 2023|website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
